entries.source_reference: Section 'A primer on linear codes', lines 304-305; Tables 1-2.
entries.value: Binary field F_2 represented as real {+1,-1}; Boolean 1 is encoded as real -1 and Boolean 0 as real +1.

entries.source_reference: Section 'A primer on linear codes', lines 340-342.
entries.value: An [n,k]_2 linear code is represented by a generator matrix G in F_2^{k x n}; codewords are xG for x in F_2^k.
Factor domains are subcodes formed from disjoint subsets of a linearly independent basis so that the global code is a direct sum/product of subcodes.

entries.source_reference: Section 'A primer on linear codes', line 304; Section 'oplus-recovery', lines 580-584.
entries.value: Binding for clean recovery is Boolean addition/XOR, implemented as point-wise multiplication in the +/-1 representation.
Input is an observation c in {+1,-1}^n and linear factor codes C_1,...,C_F.
Output is one vector c_i in each factor code C_i such that c equals XOR/bind of the recovered factors.

entries.source_reference: Theorem and proof in Section 'oplus-recovery', lines 594-606.
entries.value: Find a maximal linearly independent subset B' from the union of factor-code bases, arrange it as matrix B, solve xB = c over F_2, then reconstruct each factor from coefficients in B' intersect B_i.

entries.source_reference: Remark XORuniqueness, lines 606-608; subcode product discussion lines 368-376.
entries.value: Unique factor recovery requires the factor subcodes to behave as a direct sum/product with trivial intersections.

entries.source_reference: Remark XORuniqueness lines 606-608; decoding-with-noise caveat near end of preliminaries.
entries.value: Non-unique decomposition appears when factor subcodes overlap nontrivially; clean noise-free decoding does not cover noisy linear-code decoding.

entries.source_reference: Complexity paragraph immediately after the oplus-recovery proof.
entries.value: The clean XOR-recovery algorithm has complexity at most O(Delta^3 n), where Delta is the sum of factor-code dimensions.
The paper reports n in {500,1000,2000}, k in {3,5,7}, F in {3,4,5}, each repeated 10 times, with success defined by factor membership plus exact rebind to the observation.
The experiments were implemented with standard Python libraries including galois for F_2 computations.
